## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Kill four-mode fixture entirely. Absorb its unique per-case verification steps (summon, plumb_full, plumb_compact, layer-DiffID byte-identity round-trip, kludge tag-pair docker-inspect, abjure) into the corresponding onboarding-sequence ordain cases. Closes a current handbook-coverage gap where the ordain stubs walk only the build half but skip the verification steps the handbooks already prescribe. Also tightens foundry preflight against the airgap+empty-anchor latent bug class BBAAX exposed.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - four-mode fixture: removed entirely (manifest registration, suite registration, Rust module, dedicated test vessels and constants).
  - Onboarding-sequence ordain cases: each grows a verification tail matching the unique steps the deleted four-mode case carried for the same mode. Per-case unique-step audit lives in Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs four-mode functions (rbtdrc_fourmode_*_lifecycle); mount-time inspection identifies which steps land in which ordain case.
  - rbev-busybox vessel: removed (used only by deleted four-mode conjure_lifecycle). Other four-mode test vessels: ref-check at mount time before deleting (rbev-bottle-plantuml is needed by pluml crucible; rbev-graft-demo refs unaudited).
  - Foundry enshrine-preflight loop in rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh: airgap vessel + non-empty ORIGIN + empty ANCHOR fails-fast via the existing 404-path remediation diagnostic shape. Tether vessels keep the permissive skip (current behavior correct — buildx fetches upstream at build time).
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Onboarding-sequence keeps handbook-walk discipline. The new tail is framed as "complete the handbook walk including post-build verification" — handbooks already include these steps (rbw-Oda explicitly mentions "compare plumb" per tabtarget context) so this closes a coverage gap, not creates one.
  - Crucible fixtures stay vessel-source-agnostic per paddock's build/run split. summon/plumb do NOT move into crucible setup; they belong in the build half because the operator does them as part of completing the build cycle.
  - Gauntlet sequence shrinks by one fixture (four-mode slot removed from ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET).
  - No handbook prose changes — only test code closes the gap to existing handbook content.
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - BBAAX is the verifier — re-run gauntlet after this pace lands and confirm green tally.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Handbook prose updates (paddock framing: handbook = recipe, gauntlet = regression that runs the recipe + verifies the artifact; verification framing stays gauntlet-only).
  - Vessel directory deletion beyond rbev-busybox (audit blast radius at mount time before any other vessel delete).
  - Tabtarget/spec sweep beyond what rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh regenerates plus RBSFR fundus registry.

24e24651  ₢BBAAX    First-run inline fix for ₢BBAAX gauntlet observability gap: emit depot project ID and Cloud Build console URL via buc_info + buc_link immediately after rbgu_http_json_lro_ok succeeds creating the depot project in rbgp_depot_levy. Operator (and agent reading the live gauntlet output stream) needs the depot's Cloud Build console URL to peek at in-flight cloud builds during the long onboarding-sequence fixture; previously the URL was only emitted per-build via the existing buc_link calls in rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1021 and :1257 (after each builds.create response), leaving long quiet stretches between builds where the URL was not visible in the recent stream. Reconstructing the project ID by hand from RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX + RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER yields the wrong identifier — RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID at rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh:84 injects a 'd-' infix between the prefix and moniker (canc- + d- + canest2100004 = canc-d-canest2100004), and the agent missed this during BBAAX, sending the operator to a nonexistent project ID that produced the GCP 'You need additional access' error against canc-canest2100004 instead. Fix uses RBGC_CONSOLE_URL (already defined at rbgc_Constants.sh:78 as https://console.cloud.google.com/) suffixed with cloud-build/builds?project=${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}, matching the buc_link signature already established in rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh ('Click to ', 'Open depot Cloud Build console', URL). Did not promote ZRBFC_CLOUD_QUERY_BASE from rbfc to rbgc — the constant lives in foundry-private namespace appropriately, and rbgp's URL composition pattern at depot-levy time is short enough to inline. Preceded the buc_link with a buc_info echoing RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID itself so the project ID is grep-able from the output stream even if the link rendering is consumed by terminal styling. Insertion point is between the LRO completion and the billing-link step — the project exists in GCP at this point, all subsequent depot-levy steps continue against it, so the URL is meaningful from this moment forward. shellcheck -S error clean.                                                                 
dd5893ba  ₢BBAAX    Add Mid-execution posture rule to JJK agent context, sourced from rules-of-engagement conversation during BBAAX gauntlet first-run. Captures recurring failure mode where mid-pace surprises trigger option-proliferation responses (multi-option triage menus, scope/cost weighing, slate-vs-inline frame, follow-up-pace recommendations) that consume operator context with segmentation work that's not the agent's territory. Rule prescribes mechanism + one specific repair as default response shape; explicit prohibition on proliferating options or weighing consequences across alternatives; reaffirms that pace scope and segmentation are operator territory. Placed after Paddock posture in the agent-behavior framing cluster (Sincerity / Docket posture / Slate-time / Stale-by-mount / Docket anti-patterns / Reference discipline / Paddock posture), before Identities vs Display Names where the structural vocabulary begins. Located in jjk-claude-context.md rather than top-level CLAUDE.md because the failure mode is JJK-vocabulary-induced — outside a mount the pace-language doesn't load and the segmentation temptation doesn't surface; killing the temptation at the JJK context level is cleaner than blacklisting symptoms in a general directive.

why-GET-inside-search-loop defense, matching the existing 2-line comment style in the same function for the displayName-anchor strip). shellcheck -S error clean. 6597303f  ₢BBAAX    First-run inline fix for pristine-lifecycle/rbtdrp_depot_tear_down: zrbgp_depot_state_emit's classification path was reading state directly from CRM v3 projects:search hits, which Google documents as eventually consistent and lags mutations — notably DELETE_REQUESTED transitions post-projects.delete. After a successful rbgp_depot_unmake (which polls projects.get up to 60s waiting for DELETE_REQUESTED at the strongly-consistent endpoint), a subsequent rbgp_depot_list call observed the unmade project still as state=ACTIVE in search and emitted COMPLETE in the fact file, failing the fixture's verify. Reshape: search remains for discovery (display-name-anchored project enumeration), but per-hit state classification now issues a one-shot GET against ${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects/${projectId} — the same URL pattern rbgp_depot_unmake itself uses for verify-deletion at line 1061. Reordered loop body so projectId/displayName/moniker validation runs before the GET (skip the HTTP round trip on non-domain projects). Three new per-iteration locals (z_get_url, z_get_infix, z_get_code) declared in the BCG exception-2 block. 200 -> classify on returned .state; 404 -> skip iteration silently (project completed deletion between search and GET, fixture's absent-fact-file path treats as success); other -> log and skip, mirroring existing unrecognized-state handling. No new helper minted: surveyed rbgu_poll_until_ok (wait-for-availability), rbgu_http_json_ok (die-on-non-2xx), rbgu_http_require_ok-with-warn-code (rbgg_Governor.sh:746-750 precedent for 4xx tolerance) — none was the right shape for one-shot-read-with-skip-on-404, and only one current call site needs this composition. Inlined three primitives (rbgu_http_json + rbgu_http_code_capture + rbgu_json_field_capture) at the use site, modeled on the codebase's established posture of inlining tolerant 4xx handling rather than abstracting it. shellcheck -S error clean. Cost: 1 search call + N GET calls per list invocation (was 1 search call); for typical 4-10 depots this adds 4-10 HTTP round trips — acceptable for dev/test scale infrastructure.        
dc997d0a  ₢BBAAX    First-run inline fix for pristine-lifecycle/rbtdrp_sa_cycle: legacy governor-*.rbra search in mantle BURV output dir was stale carry-over from a prior mantle interface — current rbgp_governor_mantle deposits the RBRA at RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE (../station-files/secrets/assay/rbra.env), same staging path the retriever/director invest cases already consume from. Governor SA-cycle now mirrors the retriever/director pattern in the same function (lines 818-844): assay_canonical.exists() check + std::fs::copy(assay_canonical → governor_canonical), with assay_canonical hoisted before the governor case so all three role-cycles share one derivation. Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Kill four-mode fixture entirely. Absorb its unique per-case verification steps (summon, plumb_full, plumb_compact, layer-DiffID byte-identity round-trip, kludge tag-pair docker-inspect, abjure) into the corresponding onboarding-sequence ordain cases. Closes a current handbook-coverage gap where the ordain stubs walk only the build half but skip the verification steps the handbooks already prescribe. Also tightens foundry preflight against the airgap+empty-anchor latent bug class BBAAX exposed.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - four-mode fixture: removed entirely (manifest registration, suite registration, Rust module, dedicated test vessels and constants).
  - Onboarding-sequence ordain cases: each grows a verification tail matching the unique steps the deleted four-mode case carried for the same mode. Per-case unique-step audit lives in Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs four-mode functions (rbtdrc_fourmode_*_lifecycle); mount-time inspection identifies which steps land in which ordain case.
  - rbev-busybox vessel: removed (used only by deleted four-mode conjure_lifecycle). Other four-mode test vessels: ref-check at mount time before deleting (rbev-bottle-plantuml is needed by pluml crucible; rbev-graft-demo refs unaudited).
  - Foundry enshrine-preflight loop in rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh: airgap vessel + non-empty ORIGIN + empty ANCHOR fails-fast via the existing 404-path remediation diagnostic shape. Tether vessels keep the permissive skip (current behavior correct — buildx fetches upstream at build time).
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Onboarding-sequence keeps handbook-walk discipline. The new tail is framed as "complete the handbook walk including post-build verification" — handbooks already include these steps (rbw-Oda explicitly mentions "compare plumb" per tabtarget context) so this closes a coverage gap, not creates one.
  - Crucible fixtures stay vessel-source-agnostic per paddock's build/run split. summon/plumb do NOT move into crucible setup; they belong in the build half because the operator does them as part of completing the build cycle.
  - Gauntlet sequence shrinks by one fixture (four-mode slot removed from ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET).
  - No handbook prose changes — only test code closes the gap to existing handbook content.
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - BBAAX is the verifier — re-run gauntlet after this pace lands and confirm green tally.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Handbook prose updates (paddock framing: handbook = recipe, gauntlet = regression that runs the recipe + verifies the artifact; verification framing stays gauntlet-only).
  - Vessel directory deletion beyond rbev-busybox (audit blast radius at mount time before any other vessel delete).
  - Tabtarget/spec sweep beyond what rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh regenerates plus RBSFR fundus registry.

No new helper minted: surveyed rbgu_poll_until_ok (wait-for-availability), rbgu_http_json_ok (die-on-non-2xx), rbgu_http_require_ok-with-warn-code (rbgg_Governor.sh:746-750 precedent for 4xx tolerance) — none was the right shape for one-shot-read-with-skip-on-404, and only one current call site needs this composition. 1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	
95	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
96	
97	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
98	
99	## Suite-level fail-fast
100	
101	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
102	
103	## No-teardown decision
104	
105	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
106	
107	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
108	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
109	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
110	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
111	
112	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
113	
114	## Operator scope
115	
116	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
117	
118	## Coupling to ₣A_
119	
120	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
121	
122	## Locked design decisions
123	
124	- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
125	- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
126	- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.
127	
128	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
129	
130	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
131	
132	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
133	
134	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
135	
136	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
137	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
138	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
139	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
140	
141	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
142	
143	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
144	
145	## References
146	
147	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
148	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
149	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
150	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
151	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
152	
153	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAA7
154	
155	## Character
156	
157	Kill four-mode fixture entirely. Absorb its unique per-case verification steps (summon, plumb_full, plumb_compact, layer-DiffID byte-identity round-trip, kludge tag-pair docker-inspect, abjure) into the corresponding onboarding-sequence ordain cases. Closes a current handbook-coverage gap where the ordain stubs walk only the build half but skip the verification steps the handbooks already prescribe. Also tightens foundry preflight against the airgap+empty-anchor latent bug class BBAAX exposed.
158	
159	## Scope
160	
161	- four-mode fixture: removed entirely (manifest registration, suite registration, Rust module, dedicated test vessels and constants).
162	- Onboarding-sequence ordain cases: each grows a verification tail matching the unique steps the deleted four-mode case carried for the same mode. Per-case unique-step audit lives in Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs four-mode functions (rbtdrc_fourmode_*_lifecycle); mount-time inspection identifies which steps land in which ordain case.
163	- rbev-busybox vessel: removed (used only by deleted four-mode conjure_lifecycle). Other four-mode test vessels: ref-check at mount time before deleting (rbev-bottle-plantuml is needed by pluml crucible; rbev-graft-demo refs unaudited).
164	- Foundry enshrine-preflight loop in rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh: airgap vessel + non-empty ORIGIN + empty ANCHOR fails-fast via the existing 404-path remediation diagnostic shape. Tether vessels keep the permissive skip (current behavior correct — buildx fetches upstream at build time).
165	
166	## Constraints
167	
168	- Onboarding-sequence keeps handbook-walk discipline. The new tail is framed as "complete the handbook walk including post-build verification" — handbooks already include these steps (rbw-Oda explicitly mentions "compare plumb" per tabtarget context) so this closes a coverage gap, not creates one.
169	- Crucible fixtures stay vessel-source-agnostic per paddock's build/run split. summon/plumb do NOT move into crucible setup; they belong in the build half because the operator does them as part of completing the build cycle.
170	- Gauntlet sequence shrinks by one fixture (four-mode slot removed from ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET).
171	- No handbook prose changes — only test code closes the gap to existing handbook content.
172	
173	## Cross-pace dependencies
174	
175	- BBAAX is the verifier — re-run gauntlet after this pace lands and confirm green tally.
176	
177	## Out of scope
178	
179	- Handbook prose updates (paddock framing: handbook = recipe, gauntlet = regression that runs the recipe + verifies the artifact; verification framing stays gauntlet-only).
180	- Vessel directory deletion beyond rbev-busybox (audit blast radius at mount time before any other vessel delete).
181	- Tabtarget/spec sweep beyond what rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh regenerates plus RBSFR fundus registry.
182

# jjezs_paddock ₣BB

## Context

Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

## Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:

- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage

The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAA7

## Character

Kill four-mode fixture entirely. Absorb its unique per-case verification steps (summon, plumb_full, plumb_compact, layer-DiffID byte-identity round-trip, kludge tag-pair docker-inspect, abjure) into the corresponding onboarding-sequence ordain cases. Closes a current handbook-coverage gap where the ordain stubs walk only the build half but skip the verification steps the handbooks already prescribe. Also tightens foundry preflight against the airgap+empty-anchor latent bug class BBAAX exposed.

## Scope

- four-mode fixture: removed entirely (manifest registration, suite registration, Rust module, dedicated test vessels and constants).
- Onboarding-sequence ordain cases: each grows a verification tail matching the unique steps the deleted four-mode case carried for the same mode. Per-case unique-step audit lives in Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs four-mode functions (rbtdrc_fourmode_*_lifecycle); mount-time inspection identifies which steps land in which ordain case.
- rbev-busybox vessel: removed (used only by deleted four-mode conjure_lifecycle). Other four-mode test vessels: ref-check at mount time before deleting (rbev-bottle-plantuml is needed by pluml crucible; rbev-graft-demo refs unaudited).
- Foundry enshrine-preflight loop in rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh: airgap vessel + non-empty ORIGIN + empty ANCHOR fails-fast via the existing 404-path remediation diagnostic shape. Tether vessels keep the permissive skip (current behavior correct — buildx fetches upstream at build time).

## Constraints

- Onboarding-sequence keeps handbook-walk discipline. The new tail is framed as "complete the handbook walk including post-build verification" — handbooks already include these steps (rbw-Oda explicitly mentions "compare plumb" per tabtarget context) so this closes a coverage gap, not creates one.
- Crucible fixtures stay vessel-source-agnostic per paddock's build/run split. summon/plumb do NOT move into crucible setup; they belong in the build half because the operator does them as part of completing the build cycle.
- Gauntlet sequence shrinks by one fixture (four-mode slot removed from ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET).
- No handbook prose changes — only test code closes the gap to existing handbook content.

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBAAX is the verifier — re-run gauntlet after this pace lands and confirm green tally.

## Out of scope

- Handbook prose updates (paddock framing: handbook = recipe, gauntlet = regression that runs the recipe + verifies the artifact; verification framing stays gauntlet-only).
- Vessel directory deletion beyond rbev-busybox (audit blast radius at mount time before any other vessel delete).
- Tabtarget/spec sweep beyond what rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh regenerates plus RBSFR fundus registry.

content.text:
## Heat ₣BB — rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification (racing, 38/49)

**Paddock summary**: Pristine release qualification tier (`rbw-tP`). Refuses to run unless marshal-zero just committed. Gauntlet suite walks state ladder from zero through canonical-credentialed to crucible verification. Build/run split is load-bearing — handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay vessel-source-agnostic.

**Recent landings on BBAAX (rbw-tP-first-run)**: depot-list eventual-consistency fix (one-shot GET per hit), depot-Cloud-Build-console-URL emission at depot-levy LRO completion, governor SA-cycle aligned to assay path, BCG comment tightening. Also added "Mid-execution posture" rule to JJK context.

## Next pace — ₢BBAA7 dissolve-four-mode-and-airgap-preflight [rough]

Two coupled changes:
1. **Dissolve four-mode fixture** — remove manifest, suite, Rust module, vessels, constants. Absorb its unique per-mode verification tail (summon, plumb_full, plumb_compact, layer-DiffID byte-identity, kludge tag-pair docker-inspect, abjure) into the matching onboarding-sequence ordain cases.
2. **Airgap preflight tightening** — `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` enshrine loop fails-fast on airgap+non-empty-ORIGIN+empty-ANCHOR (the BBAAX latent bug class). Tether keeps permissive skip.

**Silks fit the docket** — both halves named, no relabel needed.

## Mount-time inspection required before approach hardens

Per docket, I need to:
- Read `rbtdrc_crucible.rs` four-mode functions (`rbtdrc_fourmode_*_lifecycle`) to enumerate which unique steps belong with which ordain mode
- Audit `rbev-bottle-plantuml` (pluml uses it — keep) and `rbev-graft-demo` refs (unaudited) before any vessel deletion
- Read current `rbtdro_onboarding.rs` ordain cases to see what's already there vs. what gets appended
- Locate `ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET` and the four-mode manifest/suite registration sites
- Read the enshrine-preflight loop in `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` and the existing 404-path remediation diagnostic to model the new failure on

## Proposed approach (initial — refine after inspection)

- **Step 1**: Inspect four-mode source + ordain source side-by-side; produce per-mode delta table (which verification tail steps land in conjure/airgap/bind/graft ordain cases)
- **Step 2**: Inspect rbev vessel directory references; confirm rbev-busybox is four-mode-only and audit rbev-graft-demo
- **Step 3**: Implement deletions (manifest, suite, module, busybox vessel, constants) + ordain-case verification tails
- **Step 4**: Add airgap preflight check in enshrine loop, modeled on existing 404-path diagnostic
- **Step 5**: Run gauntlet to confirm green (BBAAX is the verifier per cross-pace deps)

## Execution

**Sequential opus** — coordinated changes across multiple files (manifest, suite, two Rust modules, vessel dir, foundry script), with a load-bearing audit step (per-mode verification step delta) that needs careful judgment to land correctly. Not parallelizable — verification tail audit must complete before edits begin.

Ready to begin Step 1 (four-mode + ordain source inspection) and report back the per-mode delta before any edits?

content.text:
## Surface — busybox usage broader than docket anticipated

The docket says "rbev-busybox: removed (used only by deleted four-mode conjure_lifecycle)". Mount-time inspection finds **three additional live consumers**:

1. **`rbtdrc_batch_vouch_lifecycle`** (rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2941) — uses `RBTDRC_FOURMODE_CONJURE_VESSEL_DIR` to ordain a vessel for vouch state-planting
2. **`rbhocd_credential_director.sh`:45** — handbook example shows `rekon-hallmark rbev-busybox` as a "confirm live access" step
3. **`rbte_engine.sh`:49,51,64** — `four-mode` is registered in `ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE`, `ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE`, and `ZRBTE_FIXTURE_NAMES` (not just gauntlet — docket only named gauntlet)

(Spec mentions in RBSOB/RBSCB/RBS0 are historical commit-evidence — descriptive, not load-bearing on the vessel's existence.)

**Repair direction**: repoint batch-vouch to `rbev-sentry-deb-tether` (stable conjure-mode vessel, can't be removed since onboarding-sequence depends on it); update the handbook example similarly; strike `four-mode` from all suite registrations and the fixture-name list, not just gauntlet.

**Question**: Does pace ₢BBAA7 absorb this scope expansion (batch-vouch repoint + handbook example update + suite-list cleanup beyond gauntlet), or do you want to slate a separate pace for the broader busybox dissolution?

message.content: Lets strictly discuss the work of this pace.  State more clearly your concern with current docket and your finding?

content.text:
## Concern with current docket

The docket's scope statement contains two factual claims about pre-existing state that mount-time inspection contradicts:

**Claim 1 (docket):** "rbev-busybox vessel: removed (used only by deleted four-mode conjure_lifecycle)"

**Finding:** rbev-busybox has three live consumers, not one:
- `rbtdrc_fourmode_conjure_lifecycle` and `rbtdrc_fourmode_kludge_lifecycle` (the four-mode cases — being deleted, fine)
- `rbtdrc_batch_vouch_lifecycle` (rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2941) — uses `RBTDRC_FOURMODE_CONJURE_VESSEL_DIR` as its conjure vessel for vouch state-planting. **Not part of four-mode; not being deleted.** Deleting rbev-busybox breaks this fixture.
- `rbhocd_credential_director.sh`:45 — handbook step shows `rekon-hallmark rbev-busybox` as a live-access confirmation example. Deleting the vessel makes the handbook tell operators to invoke a dead reference.

**Claim 2 (docket):** "Gauntlet sequence shrinks by one fixture (four-mode slot removed from ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET)"

**Finding:** `rbte_engine.sh` registers `four-mode` in three places, not one: `ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE` (line 49), `ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE` (line 51), and `ZRBTE_FIXTURE_NAMES` (line 64). Removing only the GAUNTLET registration leaves dangling references in SERVICE and COMPLETE suites that point at a non-existent fixture.

## Nature of the concern

These aren't mid-execution implementation surprises — they're factual under-counts in the docket's scope statement made before mount. The work itself is mechanically clear; what's unclear is whether the pace's scope envelope covers the additional touchpoints (batch-vouch repoint, handbook example update, two extra suite-array cleanups).

Asking before proceeding because pace scope is operator territory.

content.text:
Partial. Your answer settles the **central question** (dissolve four-mode, but absorb the unique-to-four-mode steps into the matching onboarding ordain cases) — which is what the docket already prescribed. So the docket's intent is intact.

What it does **not** settle is whether the busybox-vessel and suite-registration cleanups land inside this pace's scope. Three open items:

1. **batch-vouch fixture's busybox dependency.** batch-vouch is independent of four-mode but reads `RBTDRC_FOURMODE_CONJURE_VESSEL_DIR`. After four-mode dissolves, either: (a) batch-vouch repoints to another conjure vessel and rbev-busybox dies, or (b) rbev-busybox stays alive solely for batch-vouch.

2. **Handbook example in rbhocd_credential_director.sh:45.** Operator-facing "live access" example showing `rekon-hallmark rbev-busybox`. Tied to (1) — if rbev-busybox dies, this example dies with it.

3. **rbte_engine.sh suite registrations.** Docket only names `ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET`, but `four-mode` is also in `ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE`, `ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE`, and `ZRBTE_FIXTURE_NAMES`. Removing the fixture without striking those leaves dangling references.

I think a useful next step before locking scope is for me to finish the **per-mode delta audit** you originally framed — exactly which four-mode steps are unique vs. already-covered in onboarding. With that table in hand we can answer concretely: does anything still need rbev-busybox, or can it dissolve fully? Want me to produce that delta now?

content.text:
## Per-mode delta audit

Source: `rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2480-2920` (four-mode) vs `rbtdro_onboarding.rs:546-940` (onboarding ordain cases).

### Conjure mode

| Layer | Four-mode (rbev-busybox) | Onboarding ordain_conjure (rbev-sentry-deb-tether) |
|-------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
| ordain | ✓ | ✓ |
| read hallmark+gar_root+ark_stem facts | ✓ | hallmark only |
| **wrest hallmark image** | ✓ | — |
| **docker run + busybox-specific output** | ✓ | — *(vessel-specific; sentry not runnable as-is)* |
| **summon (+ inspect 3 arks present)** | ✓ | — |
| **plumb_full (+ SLSA/SBOM/Dockerfile markers)** | ✓ | — |
| **rekon (image+about+vouch+attest yes)** | ✓ | — |
| **abjure** | ✓ | — |
| propagate hallmark to nameplate consumers | — | ✓ |
| git commit | — | ✓ |

**Unique-to-four-mode (absorption candidates):** wrest, summon+inspect, plumb_full+markers, rekon-all-four, abjure.
**Drop candidate:** docker-run+busybox-string assertion (vessel-specific to busybox; sentry-tether image isn't designed to print "BusyBox container is running!").
**Vessel implication:** absorbed steps are all hallmark-level operations — vessel-agnostic. rbev-busybox not needed for absorption.

### Bind mode

| Layer | Four-mode (rbev-bottle-plantuml) | Onboarding ordain_bind (rbev-bottle-plantuml — SAME) |
|-------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| ordain | ✓ | ✓ |
| read hallmark+gar_root+ark_stem | ✓ | hallmark only |
| **wrest hallmark image** | ✓ | — |
| **plumb_compact (+ "HALLMARK PLUMB:" marker + hallmark string)** | ✓ | — |
| **jettison pouch ark + rekon (pouch absent, image present)** | ✓ | — |
| **abjure** | ✓ | — |
| propagate to pluml | — | ✓ |
| git commit | — | ✓ |

**Unique-to-four-mode:** wrest, plumb_compact+marker, **surgical-jettison + post-jettison-rekon** (load-bearing supply-chain coverage — verifies surgical jettison hits only the targeted ark), abjure.
**Vessel:** identical — no vessel change needed.

### Graft mode

| Layer | Four-mode (rbev-graft-demo) | Onboarding ordain_graft (rbev-graft-demo — SAME) |
|-------|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| docker pull busybox:latest | ✓ | ✓ |
| **docker layers capture (source DiffIDs)** | ✓ | — |
| ordain with BURE_TWEAK | ✓ | ✓ |
| read hallmark+gar_root+ark_stem | ✓ | hallmark only |
| **wrest hallmark image** | ✓ | — |
| **docker layers capture (wrested DiffIDs)** | ✓ | — |
| **compare source == wrested (DiffID round-trip)** | ✓ | — *(LOAD-BEARING — graft byte-identity contract)* |
| **abjure** | ✓ | — |
| git commit | — | ✓ |

**Unique-to-four-mode:** layer-DiffID round-trip is the load-bearing graft contract — proves graft pushes bytes unchanged through GAR's manifest-envelope normalization. Plus wrest, abjure.
**Vessel:** identical — no vessel change needed.

### Kludge mode

| Layer | Four-mode (rbev-busybox) | Onboarding kludge_tadmor/ccyolo (nameplate-level) |
|-------|--------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| **vessel-level kludge (rbw-fk LocalKludge)** | ✓ | — *(uses rbw-cKB/rbw-cKS — nameplate-level wrappers)* |
| read hallmark+gar_root+ark_stem | ✓ | — |
| **docker inspect image+vouch tags locally** | ✓ | — |
| **docker run + busybox output** | ✓ | — *(vessel-specific)* |
| nameplate-level kludge (rbw-cKB+rbw-cKS) | — | ✓ |
| git commit | — | ✓ |

**Coverage gap:** vessel-level kludge primitive (`rbw-fk`) has no test coverage in onboarding — onboarding only tests the nameplate-level wrappers. If rbw-fk's tag-pair behavior is the same machinery as rbw-cKB internals, it's covered transitively. If not, dropping the four-mode kludge case loses coverage of rbw-fk's vessel-direct invocation.

## Vessel disposition implications

- **rbev-busybox**: needed by four-mode conjure + kludge AND batch-vouch fixture. Absorbed onboarding tail is vessel-agnostic, so absorption itself doesn't keep busybox alive. Question becomes: does batch-vouch repoint, or does busybox stay for batch-vouch?
- **rbev-bottle-plantuml**: shared with onboarding — no change.
- **rbev-graft-demo**: shared with onboarding — no change.

## Net summary

Three modes (conjure, bind, graft) absorb cleanly: append a verification tail (wrest + mode-specific verification + abjure) to the existing onboarding ordain case. Kludge mode is the only place with potential coverage gap (vessel-level vs nameplate-level kludge primitive); needs a brief check of whether rbw-cKB internally invokes rbw-fk machinery.

Where do you want to land on (a) the conjure-mode docker-run+busybox-string assertion (drop or relocate?), (b) the kludge-mode vessel-level coverage (drop, absorb into onboarding kludge cases, or preserve as a small standalone fixture?), and (c) batch-vouch's busybox dependency (repoint or keep busybox alive for batch-vouch)?

content.text:
Yes. Recommendation:

**1. Absorb conjure/bind/graft verification tails into onboarding ordain cases as the docket prescribed.** Per delta, the absorbed steps (wrest, summon+inspect, plumb_full/compact, rekon, jettison+rekon-after, DiffID round-trip, abjure) are all hallmark-level — vessel-agnostic. They land cleanly without changing onboarding's vessel choices.

**2. Drop the docker-run+busybox-string assertions in conjure and kludge.** They're busybox-specific runtime checks; per-crucible fixtures (tadmor, srjcl, pluml, moriah) already cover "image runs and behaves" against vessels designed for it. The supply-chain assertions (wrest/summon/plumb/rekon) are what's load-bearing, and those carry over.

**3. Investigate rbw-fk vs rbw-cKB before deciding kludge.** If `rbw-cKB` invokes the same vessel-kludge machinery as `rbw-fk` (likely — nameplate kludge usually wraps vessel kludge), the four-mode kludge case is transitively covered and drops cleanly. If they're independent code paths, preserve it as a one-case mini-fixture against a non-busybox vessel. I'll verify in 1-2 file reads before deciding.

**4. Treat busybox cleanup as in-scope chain consequence, not scope creep.** Once four-mode dies and the absorption is vessel-agnostic, the only thing keeping rbev-busybox alive is batch-vouch and the handbook example. Both are mechanical one-line changes (repoint batch-vouch's vessel constant to `rbev-sentry-deb-tether`; update handbook example to a vessel that exists). The `rbte_engine.sh` suite-name strike is also mechanical. None of these are design decisions — they're the necessary follow-through of "dissolve four-mode entirely". Keeping busybox alive solely for batch-vouch's incidental dependency would be a worse outcome than completing the dissolution.

**5. Airgap enshrine-preflight (docket part 2) is unaffected by all of the above.** Standalone change in `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh`.

Net pace shape: dissolve four-mode (manifest, suite-name in 4 places, Rust module, vessel directory rbev-busybox); absorb three verification tails into onboarding; investigate-then-decide on kludge; mechanical follow-through on batch-vouch repoint + handbook example; airgap preflight tightening.

rbob_kludge() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  zrbfc_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Build bottle vessel locally and drive hallmark into nameplate"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_step "Kludge: ${RBRN_MONIKER} (${RBRN_BOTTLE_VESSEL})"

  # Delegate build to foundry kludge — override BUZ_FOLIO with the vessel sigil
  # for the duration of the call (rbfd_kludge reads BUZ_FOLIO as its operand).
  BUZ_FOLIO="${RBRN_BOTTLE_VESSEL}" rbfd_kludge

  # Read hallmark from fact file
  local z_hallmark=""
  z_hallmark=$(<"${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}") \
    || buc_die "Failed to read hallmark from kludge output"
  test -n "${z_hallmark}" || buc_die "Empty hallmark from kludge output"

  # Drive hallmark into nameplate
  zrbob_drive_hallmark "${ZRBOB_ENV_RBRN}" "RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK" "${z_hallmark}"

  buc_success "Kludge installed: ${z_hallmark} → ${RBRN_MONIKER}"
}

## rbw-fk vs rbw-cKB/rbw-cKS — transitivity confirmed

`rbob_kludge_bottle` (rbob_bottle.sh:481) and `rbob_kludge_sentry` (rbob_bottle.sh:517) both delegate the actual build to `rbfd_kludge` (lines 500, 526) — the exact same function that `rbw-fk` invokes directly. The nameplate-level wrappers add only:
- sentinel checks
- `BUZ_FOLIO` override (vessel sigil)
- hallmark fact read
- `zrbob_drive_hallmark` into nameplate's rbrn.env

So four-mode's kludge case (which invokes `rbw-fk` directly on rbev-busybox) has no unique coverage of `rbfd_kludge` machinery — onboarding's `kludge_tadmor` and `kludge_ccyolo` cases exercise the same code path through the nameplate wrappers.

The "unique" steps in four-mode kludge were:
- `docker inspect` of image+vouch tags → produced by `rbfd_kludge` regardless of caller (transitively covered)
- `docker run` + busybox-string assertion → vessel-specific (drop per recommendation #2)

**Conclusion: four-mode kludge case drops cleanly. No mini-fixture needed.**

content.text:
Before I start coding, I hit two design decisions while planning the absorption that I want your call on:

**Q1: Abjure in absorbed tails.** Four-mode cases end with abjure (delete the hallmark from GAR). But onboarding has downstream consumers — the per-crucible fixtures (tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) charge containers using the propagated hallmarks. Abjuring during onboarding would break those downstream fixtures. Plus the docket's no-teardown decision says "After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection."

My read: drop `abjure` from conjure and bind absorbed tails (would mutate GAR state needed downstream). Keep abjure in graft tail only (graft-demo is terminal, no consumers). Net effect: graft mode covers the abjure path; conjure/bind paths lose explicit abjure coverage in the gauntlet (still exercised by `rbw-fA` outside the suite).

**Q2: Bind's surgical pouch jettison.** Four-mode bind case jettisons the pouch ark and uses rekon to verify pouch-absent + image-present (selective-jettison correctness test). This mutates GAR state — deletes the pouch ark from the plantuml hallmark. Need to know whether downstream pluml fixture (which charges a crucible from this plantuml hallmark) needs the pouch ark, or only the image ark.

Let me check that quickly before asking:

message.content: Your point about abjure is actually a critical finding.  I need four-mode to keep one busybox build built in the cloud (airgap or tether, doesn't matter; tether simpler?) so that we can indeed test that abjure deletes all the pieces-parts of an ark: all images must be deleted.  That is an existing test case in four-mode, right?

content.text:
**Critical finding — no, that test case does not exist in four-mode.**

I checked all three abjure-bearing four-mode cases (conjure, bind, graft). Each one calls abjure and checks its exit code is 0:

```rust
match rbtdri_invoke_global(ctx, RBTDRM_COLOPHON_ABJURE, &[&hallmark], &[...]) {
    Ok(r) if r.exit_code == 0 => {}
    Ok(r) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("abjure failed (exit {}) ...
```

But **none of them perform a post-abjure rekon** to verify the image/about/vouch/attest/pouch arks are actually gone from GAR. They trust the exit code.

Compare to four-mode bind's surgical pouch-jettison test (rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2710-2729) — that DOES post-jettison-rekon to verify the pouch is gone and the image survived. That's the shape of test that's missing for abjure: a post-abjure rekon asserting all five basenames now absent.

So what you want is a coverage step that doesn't exist yet — not "preserve" but "add". The minimal new shape:

1. Ordain a hallmark in conjure-tether mode (simpler than airgap — no enshrine prereq)
2. Rekon → assert image+about+vouch+attest+pouch all `yes`
3. Abjure
4. Rekon → assert image+about+vouch+attest+pouch all absent (or row not present at all)

Two questions before I shape the work:

1. **Where does this case live?** Options:
   - A: A small surviving four-mode-derived fixture with one case (call it `abjure-completeness` or similar; uses rbev-busybox; keeps busybox alive)
   - B: A new section/case inside an existing fixture (batch-vouch already does the "one cloud-built busybox hallmark" pattern; could grow a section)
   - C: Append as the terminal step of the absorbed onboarding ordain_graft tail (graft-demo is terminal; we already abjure there; just add post-abjure rekon)

2. **Does graft-mode abjure suffice for the coverage you want, or does it need to be conjure-mode specifically?** Graft has fewer ark types (no pouch — "graft's missing pouch" per rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh:410), so it doesn't exercise the full ark inventory. Conjure does. If the test is to assert "all ark types abjure cleanly", conjure-mode is the right vehicle.

If the answer is "conjure-mode, comprehensive coverage", then option A or B is the right shape, busybox stays, and the busybox cleanup chain (batch-vouch repoint + handbook example update) gets cancelled.

content.text:
Comprehensive search complete. Abjure has **four call sites** in theurge, all using the same shape (check exit code only, no post-abjure assertion):

| Location | Fixture | Verifies ark removal? |
|----------|---------|----------------------|
| `rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2619` | four-mode conjure | No — exit code only |
| `rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2736` | four-mode bind | No — exit code only |
| `rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2836` | four-mode graft | No — exit code only |
| `rbtdrc_crucible.rs:3019` | batch-vouch | No — exit code only |

Notable absences (none of these have any abjure call):
- `rbtdro_onboarding.rs` (onboarding-sequence)
- `rbtdrf_fast.rs`, `rbtdrf_handbook.rs`
- `rbtdrk_canonical.rs` (canonical-establish)
- `rbtdrp_pristine.rs` (pristine-lifecycle)
- `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (calibrant family)
- All test modules (`rbtdt*`)

**Conclusion**: there is no existing test in the entire theurge suite that verifies abjure completeness. The closest precedent is four-mode bind's surgical pouch-jettison test (rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2710-2729) which does post-jettison rekon — same shape your proposed abjure-completeness test would take, but applied to a different operation.

So your finding stands as an **identified coverage gap**, not a relocate-the-existing-test exercise. Whatever survives needs to actually add the post-abjure rekon assertion — that work hasn't been done before.

Given that, want me to shape the surviving four-mode-derived case as: ordain-conjure-tether on rbev-busybox → rekon (assert all 5 basenames `yes`) → abjure → rekon (assert all 5 basenames absent)? With this alive, batch-vouch keeps using busybox and the busybox-cleanup chain (handbook example, batch-vouch repoint, rbev-busybox deletion) is cancelled.

content.text:
## Proposed shape for the surviving case

```
Case: ark-lifecycle-conjure (or other name — see Q1)

1. Probe:    director RBRA present (canonical-establish ran)
2. List:     rbw-iah → save baseline hallmark list (write to step file)
3. Ordain:   rbev-busybox in conjure mode (rbw-fO)
             — capture new hallmark from BURV facts
4. List:     rbw-iah → assert == baseline ∪ {new_hallmark}
5. Rekon:    new_hallmark → assert all 5 basenames (image, about, vouch, attest, pouch) == yes
6. Abjure:   new_hallmark (rbw-fA)
7. List:     rbw-iah → assert == baseline (NEW hallmark gone AND no collateral damage to others)
```

Step 7 carries the load — the baseline-diff catches both "abjure removed our hallmark" AND "abjure didn't touch anything else". Rekon at step 5 establishes that ordain actually produced all 5 ark basenames so step 7's removal proof is meaningful.

**Disposition:** Independent, single section, single case.
**Suite memberships:** same as current four-mode — SERVICE, COMPLETE, GAUNTLET, FIXTURE_NAMES.
**Position in gauntlet:** slot 6 (where four-mode currently sits).
**Source location:** stay in `rbtdrc_crucible.rs` (replaces the old four-mode section — preserves locality with the rekon/docker helpers and rbev-busybox vessel constant).

## Three questions before I code

**Q1 (naming):** What do we call the fixture? My recommendations:
- `ark-lifecycle` — accurate scope; room for sibling cases later
- `abjure-completeness` — narrower; describes the load-bearing assertion

**Q2 (post-abjure rekon):** Should I also try `rbw-irh` (DirectorRekonsHallmark) on the abjured hallmark and assert it returns no-yes-rows / fails / something specific? Or skip — the `rbw-iah` baseline-diff is already proof and rekon-on-deleted-hallmark may have undefined behavior. My recommendation: skip rekon at step 7. The list-diff is the cleaner contract.

**Q3 (busybox-cleanup chain reversal):** With this case alive, rbev-busybox stays. That cancels the busybox-related cleanups from the prior plan. To confirm: 
- batch-vouch keeps `RBTDRC_FOURMODE_CONJURE_VESSEL_DIR` reference (rename the constant to something like `RBTDRC_BUSYBOX_VESSEL_DIR` since it's no longer "fourmode-only", but the vessel reference stands)
- rbhocd_credential_director.sh:45 handbook example stays as-is (`rekon-hallmark rbev-busybox`)
- rbev-vessels/rbev-busybox/ directory stays
- The other four-mode constants (`RBTDRC_FOURMODE_BIND_VESSEL_DIR`, `RBTDRC_FOURMODE_GRAFT_VESSEL_DIR`, `RBTDRC_FOURMODE_GRAFT_SOURCE_IMAGE`, `RBTDRC_FOURMODE_BUSYBOX_RUN_OUTPUT`) all delete since the corresponding cases get absorbed into onboarding (no busybox-run assertion, no graft constants needed since onboarding's graft case has its own)

Confirm chain reversal and answer the two naming/scope questions, then I code.

file.content:
# Theurge-own colophons (routed by rbte_dispatch, not the RBK zipper)
  readonly ZRBTE_COLOPHONS="rbtd-ap"

  # Combined manifest: RBK zipper colophons + theurge-own colophons
  readonly ZRBTE_FULL_MANIFEST="${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST} ${ZRBTE_COLOPHONS}"

  # Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=("enrollment-validation" "regime-validation" "regime-smoke" "handbook-render")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "four-mode" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "four-mode" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "four-mode"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

rbte_build() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  buc_success "Theurge built"
}

2379	/// Docker wrapper: run image and capture output.
2380	fn rbtdrc_docker_run(image_ref: &str) -> Result<(String, i32), String> {
2381	    let output = Command::new("docker")
2382	        .args(["run", "--rm", image_ref])
2383	        .output()
2384	        .map_err(|e| format!("docker run exec failed: {}", e))?;
2385	    let stdout = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout).into_owned();
2386	    let stderr = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).into_owned();
2387	    let combined = if stderr.is_empty() {
2388	        stdout
2389	    } else {
2390	        format!("{}{}", stdout, stderr)
2391	    };
2392	    Ok((combined, output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1)))
2393	}
2394	
2395	/// Docker wrapper: inspect image (returns true if exists).
2396	fn rbtdrc_docker_inspect(image_ref: &str) -> bool {
2397	    Command::new("docker")
2398	        .args(["inspect", image_ref])
2399	        .stdout(Stdio::null())
2400	        .stderr(Stdio::null())
2401	        .status()
2402	        .map(|s| s.success())
2403	        .unwrap_or(false)
2404	}
2405	
2406	/// Docker wrapper: remove images.
2407	fn rbtdrc_docker_rmi(refs: &[&str]) -> Result<(), String> {
2408	    let status = Command::new("docker")
2409	        .arg("rmi")
2410	        .args(refs)
2411	        .stdout(Stdio::null())
2412	        .stderr(Stdio::null())
2413	        .status()
2414	        .map_err(|e| format!("docker rmi exec failed: {}", e))?;
2415	    if !status.success() {
2416	        return Err(format!("docker rmi exited {}", status.code().unwrap_or(-1)));
2417	    }
2418	    Ok(())
2419	}
