293	  buc_step "Verifying enshrined base images exist in GAR"
294	
295	  local z_n=""
296	  local z_anchor_var=""
297	  local z_anchor=""
298	  local z_pkg_path=""
299	  local z_tag=""
300	  local z_origin_var=""
301	  local z_origin=""
302	  local z_any_checked="false"
303	  local z_status_file=""
304	  local z_response_file=""
305	  local z_stderr_file=""
306	  local z_http_code=""
307	
308	  for z_n in 1 2 3; do
309	    z_origin_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ORIGIN"
310	    z_anchor_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ANCHOR"
311	    z_origin="${!z_origin_var:-}"
312	    z_anchor="${!z_anchor_var:-}"
313	
314	    # Skip slots without an origin or without an anchor (pass-through images don't need enshrine)
315	    test -n "${z_origin}" || continue
316	    test -n "${z_anchor}" || continue
317	
318	    case "${z_anchor}" in
319	      *:*) : ;;
320	      *)   buc_die "Invalid ${z_anchor_var} locator format (expected package-path:tag): ${z_anchor}" ;;
321	    esac
322	    z_pkg_path="${z_anchor%:*}"
323	    z_tag="${z_anchor##*:}"
324	    test -n "${z_pkg_path}" || buc_die "Package path is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"
325	    test -n "${z_tag}"      || buc_die "Tag is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"
326	
327	    z_any_checked="true"
328	    z_status_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_status.txt"
329	    z_response_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_response.txt"
330	    z_stderr_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_stderr.txt"
331	
332	    curl --head -sS \
333	      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
334	      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}" \
335	      -H "Authorization: Bearer ${z_token}" \
336	      -H "Accept: ${ZRBFC_ACCEPT_MANIFEST_MTYPES}" \
337	      -w "%{http_code}" \
338	      -o "${z_response_file}" \
339	      "${ZRBFC_REGISTRY_API_BASE}/${z_pkg_path}/manifests/${z_tag}" \
340	      > "${z_status_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
341	      || buc_die "HEAD request failed for enshrined image: ${z_anchor} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
342	
343	    z_http_code=$(<"${z_status_file}")
344	    test -n "${z_http_code}" || buc_die "HTTP status code is empty for enshrine check"
345	
346	    if test "${z_http_code}" = "404"; then
347	      buc_warn "Enshrined base image not found: ${z_anchor} (from ${z_origin})"
348	      buc_bare "  Enshrine copies upstream base images (e.g., busybox:latest from Docker Hub) into"

file.content:
buc_step "Verifying enshrined base images exist in GAR"

  local z_n=""
  local z_anchor_var=""
  local z_anchor=""
  local z_pkg_path=""
  local z_tag=""
  local z_origin_var=""
  local z_origin=""
  local z_any_checked="false"
  local z_status_file=""
  local z_response_file=""
  local z_stderr_file=""
  local z_http_code=""

  for z_n in 1 2 3; do
    z_origin_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ORIGIN"
    z_anchor_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ANCHOR"
    z_origin="${!z_origin_var:-}"
    z_anchor="${!z_anchor_var:-}"

    # Skip slots without an origin or without an anchor (pass-through images don't need enshrine)
    test -n "${z_origin}" || continue
    test -n "${z_anchor}" || continue

    case "${z_anchor}" in
      *:*) : ;;
      *)   buc_die "Invalid ${z_anchor_var} locator format (expected package-path:tag): ${z_anchor}" ;;
    esac
    z_pkg_path="${z_anchor%:*}"
    z_tag="${z_anchor##*:}"
    test -n "${z_pkg_path}" || buc_die "Package path is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"
    test -n "${z_tag}"      || buc_die "Tag is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"

    z_any_checked="true"
    z_status_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_status.txt"
    z_response_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_response.txt"
    z_stderr_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_stderr.txt"

    curl --head -sS \
      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}" \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer ${z_token}" \
      -H "Accept: ${ZRBFC_ACCEPT_MANIFEST_MTYPES}" \
      -w "%{http_code}" \
      -o "${z_response_file}" \
      "${ZRBFC_REGISTRY_API_BASE}/${z_pkg_path}/manifests/${z_tag}" \
      > "${z_status_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
      || buc_die "HEAD request failed for enshrined image: ${z_anchor} — see ${z_stderr_file}"

    z_http_code=$(<"${z_status_file}")
    test -n "${z_http_code}" || buc_die "HTTP status code is empty for enshrine check"

    if test "${z_http_code}" = "404"; then
      buc_warn "Enshrined base image not found: ${z_anchor} (from ${z_origin})"
      buc_bare "  Enshrine copies upstream base images (e.g., busybox:latest from Docker Hub) into"
      buc_bare "  your private GAR, pinned by content hash. Like the reliquary, this ensures"
      buc_bare "  air-gapped builds never reach the public internet. The anchor locator is stable"
      buc_bare "  until you deliberately re-enshrine to pick up a newer upstream version."
      buc_bare "  Multiple vessels sharing the same base image anchor need only one enshrine."
      buc_bare "  Run enshrine, then re-run ordain:"
      buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_ENSHRINE_VESSEL}" "${z_vessel_dir}"
      buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_ORDAIN_HALLMARK}" "${z_vessel_dir}"
      buc_die "Registry preflight failed — enshrined base image missing from GAR"
    elif test "${z_http_code}" != "200"; then
      buc_die "Unexpected HTTP ${z_http_code} when checking enshrined image: ${z_anchor}"
    fi

    buc_log_args "Enshrined image verified: ${z_anchor}"
  done

  if test "${z_any_checked}" = "true"; then
    buc_info "All enshrined base images verified in GAR"
  fi
}

input.old_string:
# Skip slots without an origin or without an anchor (pass-through images don't need enshrine)
    test -n "${z_origin}" || continue
    test -n "${z_anchor}" || continue
input.new_string:
# Skip slots without an origin (no base image to enshrine).
    test -n "${z_origin}" || continue

    # Anchor handling depends on egress mode. Tether passes through (buildx
    # fetches upstream at build time). Airgap requires a populated anchor
    # because the worker pool has no upstream egress — an empty anchor with
    # a non-empty origin guarantees runtime build failure.
    if test -z "${z_anchor}"; then
      if test "${RBRV_EGRESS_MODE:-}" = "airgap"; then
        buc_warn "Airgap vessel ${RBRV_SIGIL} has empty ${z_anchor_var} but non-empty ${z_origin_var}=${z_origin}"
        buc_bare "  Airgap conjure cannot reach upstream — base images must be enshrined first."
        buc_bare "  The anchor locator points at the enshrined image inside GAR. Without it,"
        buc_bare "  the airgap worker pool has no source for the base image and the build fails."
        buc_bare "  Run enshrine, then re-run ordain:"
        buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_ENSHRINE_VESSEL}" "${z_vessel_dir}"
        buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_ORDAIN_HALLMARK}" "${z_vessel_dir}"
        buc_die "Registry preflight failed — airgap vessel missing required anchor"
      fi
      continue
    fi

content.text:
All work complete and verified at the levels possible without running cloud spend.

## Summary of changes

**Code (8 files, 498/500 net):**
- `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`: `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_FOUR_MODE` → `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_HALLMARK_LIFECYCLE`; required-colophons table — hallmark-lifecycle entry (4 colophons: ORDAIN, ABJURE, REKON_HALLMARK, AUDIT_HALLMARKS); onboarding-sequence entry grew 6→13 colophons (for absorbed tails)
- `rbtdtm_manifest.rs`: matching test rename
- `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`: deleted 4 fourmode functions + SECTIONS_FOUR_MODE + 4 obsolete constants + 2 obsolete docker helpers (~430 lines); added new `rbtdrc_hallmark_lifecycle` case + `zrbtdrc_audit_parse` helper + `RBTDRC_BUSYBOX_VESSEL_DIR` (~150 lines); promoted ark/fact constants and 3 docker helpers to `pub(crate)`
- `rbtdro_onboarding.rs`: added `rbtdro_ordain_capture_full` (returns hallmark+gar_root+ark_stem); absorbed verification tails into ordain_conjure (wrest+summon+plumb_full+rekon — no abjure), ordain_bind (wrest+plumb_compact+jettison-pouch+rekon-after — no abjure), ordain_graft (capture-source-layers+wrest+capture-wrested-layers+compare+abjure — terminal so abjure safe); removed local duplicates of GAR_CATEGORY_HALLMARKS and ARK_BASENAME_IMAGE
- `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh`: airgap+empty-ANCHOR fail-fast in enshrine-preflight loop, modeled on existing 404-path remediation diagnostic
- `rbte_engine.sh`: `four-mode` → `hallmark-lifecycle` in SUITE_SERVICE, SUITE_COMPLETE, SUITE_GAUNTLET (3 places)
- `tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.four-mode.sh` → `tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.hallmark-lifecycle.sh`; same for `rbtd-s.FixtureCase.*`
- `CLAUDE.md`: service suite count updated

**Verification:**
- `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` clean (1.19s)
- `tt/rbtd-t.Test.sh` clean (91 unit tests pass)
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` clean (4 fixtures, 75 cases including handbook-render which exercises rbhocd_credential_director.sh — confirms busybox-handbook-example reference still valid)
- shellcheck clean on rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh
- `tt/rbw-MG.MarshalGenerate.sh` ran clean — no tabtarget context drift (zipper enrollments unchanged)

**Pending — operator-initiated (per pristine-tier contract):**
- Full gauntlet run on a release branch from marshal-zero state. Per docket cross-pace dep, BBAAX is the verifier; you decide when to fire it.

**Surfaced for your call (paddock update — operator territory):**
- `.claude/jjm/jjp_uBuB.md` lines 67 ("6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)") and 91 (lists `four-mode` in Independent fixtures) describe shape that's now stale. JJK paddock posture says paddocks describe shape, not history — these would benefit from rename to `hallmark-lifecycle`. Not done autonomously since paddock edits are normally operator-driven.

Tasks #1–7 all complete.

content.text:
Preconditions verified — director RBRA present, canonical depot exists (`canest2100004`), rbev-busybox has reliquary stamp `r260503141803` yoked in. Fixture should run cleanly.

**Quick risk read:**
- Cost: ~$0.05 of Cloud Build, ~5-10 min runtime (busybox is small/fast)
- Side effects: ordains a hallmark in GAR, then abjures it. If abjure fails midway, leaves a stranded hallmark needing manual `rbw-fA` cleanup
- Untested code surfaces here: the audit-parse helper and the post-abjure rekon assertion (per your earlier guidance: fail loud, debug)

No reason not to run.

Notching first per your direction. Then running.

params.intent: Dissolve four-mode fixture into hallmark-lifecycle (single load-bearing case: ordain rbev-busybox in conjure mode, audit baseline-diff to detect collateral damage, rekon all 5 ark basenames present, abjure, post-abjure rekon assert all 5 absent, final audit assert baseline restored). Replaces the 4 four-mode cases (conjure/bind/graft/kludge) which only checked abjure exit code without verifying ark removal. The unique verification value of the dissolved cases absorbs into onboarding-sequence ordain cases as verification tails (no abjure for conjure/bind since downstream per-crucible fixtures consume those hallmarks; abjure kept in graft tail since graft-demo is terminal). Kludge case drops cleanly (rbw-cKB/rbw-cKS delegate to the same rbfd_kludge machinery rbw-fk invokes, transitively covered by onboarding nameplate-kludge cases). docker-run+busybox-string assertions drop as vessel-specific noise (per-crucible fixtures cover image-runs-correctly against vessels designed for it). rbev-busybox vessel preserved (used by surviving hallmark-lifecycle case AND batch-vouch fixture); RBTDRC_FOURMODE_CONJURE_VESSEL_DIR renamed to RBTDRC_BUSYBOX_VESSEL_DIR. Onboarding tails added: ordain_conjure grows wrest+summon+inspect-3-arks+plumb_full+rekon-4-basenames+rmi (no abjure); ordain_bind grows wrest+plumb_compact+jettison-pouch+rekon-after-jettison+rmi (no abjure, surgical jettison preserves image for downstream pluml); ordain_graft grows source-layer-capture+wrest+wrested-layer-capture+DiffID-compare+rmi+abjure (terminal graft-demo, abjure safe). Helper rbtdro_ordain_capture_full added for tails needing gar_root+ark_stem facts beyond hallmark. RCG cleanup: pub(crate) on RBTDRC_FACT_GAR_ROOT/ARK_STEM/ARK_BASENAME_*/GAR_CATEGORY_HALLMARKS and helpers rbtdrc_docker_inspect/rmi/layers_capture/rekon_basename_yes (now consumed by onboarding tails); rbtdrc_docker_run and rbtdrc_docker_pull deleted (only deleted four-mode used them); local duplicates RBTDRO_GAR_CATEGORY_HALLMARKS and RBTDRO_ARK_BASENAME_IMAGE in onboarding removed in favor of rbtdrc imports. Manifest: RBTDRM_FIXTURE_FOUR_MODE renamed to RBTDRM_FIXTURE_HALLMARK_LIFECYCLE, required-colophons table entry narrowed (4 colophons: ORDAIN, ABJURE, REKON_HALLMARK, AUDIT_HALLMARKS); onboarding-sequence required-colophons grew 6→13 to cover absorbed-tail invocations. rbte_engine.sh: 'four-mode' renamed to 'hallmark-lifecycle' in 3 suite arrays (SERVICE, COMPLETE, GAUNTLET). Tabtargets: rbtd-r.FixtureRun.four-mode.sh and rbtd-s.FixtureCase.four-mode.sh renamed to .hallmark-lifecycle.sh siblings. Airgap enshrine-preflight tightening (BBAAX-exposed bug class): rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh enshrine-preflight loop now fails-fast when RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=airgap with non-empty origin and empty anchor (previously skipped silently, guaranteeing runtime build failure deeper in the pipeline). ```

```

The two big files (rbtdrc_crucible.rs and rbtdro_onboarding.rs together = 55,039 bytes) are inseparable: the rbtdrc constants/helpers I made `pub(crate)` only have consumers because of the onboarding tail edits, and vice versa. Splitting them across commits would leave one or the other failing the build.

**Two paths forward:**

1. **Raise the limit for this commit** (one-shot `size_limit` override on the jjx_record call). Justified because the work is one logical unit — the four-mode dissolution is incoherent if split across the consumer/producer boundary.

2. **Split into two commits, accepting the broken-build interim**: commit (a) rbtdrc changes (constants + helpers + new fixture + delete fourmode) — would fail to build because made-pub items have no consumers yet; commit (b) the rest (onboarding tails + manifest + bash + tabtargets) — restores green. Commit (a) is broken-by-design.

Recommend path 1 — single commit preserves bisectability and reflects the work as actually done. Raise to ~70000 bytes for headroom.

Which?

# Tool Image Reliquary

# Conjuring Configuration

content.text:
**Finding — the new airgap-preflight fired on rbev-busybox itself.**

The vessel config at `rbev-vessels/rbev-busybox/rbrv.env`:
```
RBRV_VESSEL_MODE=conjure
RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=airgap          ← the trip wire
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=busybox:latest
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR=             ← empty
```

This is exactly the bug class BBAAX exposed and that my new preflight is designed to catch. **The preflight is working correctly.** The vessel itself is in an inconsistent state — declared airgap (no upstream egress allowed) but no enshrined base image to draw from.

How did the old four-mode tests work against this vessel? The previous preflight silently skipped on empty anchor; the subsequent Cloud Build would either succeed because of stale enshrinement state, or fail deeper with a confusing error. My preflight surfaces the real bug.

Two options to make rbev-busybox usable:
1. **Switch to tether** (`RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=tether`) — buildx fetches `busybox:latest` from Docker Hub at build time, no enshrine needed. Simplest, matches your earlier "tether simpler?" framing
2. **Enshrine first** — operator runs `tt/rbw-dE.DirectorEnshrinesVessel.sh rbev-busybox`, which populates the anchor; then ordain proceeds. Keeps airgap coverage in this vessel

Recommendation: option 1 (tether). The hallmark-lifecycle case is mode-agnostic — it just needs a full conjure that produces 5 ark basenames. Airgap-mode coverage is already exercised by `onboarding-sequence/ordain_airgap` (using rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap, properly enshrined).

Single-line change to rbev-busybox/rbrv.env. batch-vouch (the other rbev-busybox consumer) doesn't care about egress mode either.

Want me to flip rbev-busybox to tether and re-run? Or take a different path?

message.content: Switch to tether now, notch, and then retry

#
# Recipe Bottle Regime Vessel - BusyBox (Conjuring)

# Core Identity
RBRV_SIGIL=rbev-busybox
RBRV_DESCRIPTION="Busybox small image for proving builds are working well cheaply"

# Vessel Mode
RBRV_VESSEL_MODE=conjure

# Egress Mode
RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=airgap

# Tool Image Reliquary
RBRV_RELIQUARY=r260503141803

# Conjuring Configuration
RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE="${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/${RBRV_SIGIL}/Dockerfile"
RBRV_CONJURE_BLDCONTEXT="${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/${RBRV_SIGIL}"
RBRV_CONJURE_PLATFORMS="linux/amd64 linux/arm64 linux/arm/v7"

# Base Images (ARG/FROM pattern — resolved by Foundry at conjure time)
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=busybox:latest
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR=

# eof

params.intent: Switch rbev-busybox from airgap to tether egress mode. The vessel was misconfigured as airgap with empty RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR — an inconsistent state where ordain has no source for the base image. The new airgap enshrine-preflight (added earlier in this pace) correctly fired on this and refused to ordain. Previous four-mode tests worked against the vessel only because the prior preflight silently skipped empty anchors, leaving the runtime build to fail or succeed depending on stale enshrinement state in GAR. tether mode is the correct shape for this vessel's role: a small fast cloud-built hallmark used by hallmark-lifecycle (ordain/abjure round-trip coverage) and batch-vouch (vouch state-planting). Neither consumer cares about egress mode; tether lets buildx fetch busybox:latest from Docker Hub at build time without enshrine prerequisite. Airgap-mode coverage stays in onboarding-sequence/ordain_airgap (rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap, properly enshrined via the airgap chain that lands in this pace's absorbed onboarding tails).

lines: +/// Recognize a hallmark identifier — shape is `<lowercase letter><digits>-r<digits>`
+/// (mode-letter + creation timestamp + reliquary stamp). Used to positively
+/// match hallmark rows out of audit stdout, robust to interleaved BUW banner,
+/// ANSI-coded log lines, and table chrome.
/// Parse `rbw-iah` audit-hallmarks stdout and return a sorted Vec of hallmark
-/// identifiers. The audit prints a small table with two-space-indented rows;
-/// header ("HALLMARK", separator dashes) and "Total hallmarks: N" footer are
+/// identifiers. Positively matches hallmark-shaped lines; everything else
+/// (BUW pre-banner, ANSI-coded buc_step logs, table chrome, totals) is ignored.

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# BUF Fact - Dual-write fact-file primitives
#
# Writes fact-files to both BURD_OUTPUT_DIR and BURD_TEMP_DIR.
# BURD_OUTPUT_DIR is cleared on next dispatch (latest-command convenience).
# BURD_TEMP_DIR is durable (addressable by remote consumers).

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard (return 0 — sourced from non-BCG dispatch context)
test -z "${ZBUF_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZBUF_SOURCED=1

# Tinder constants (pure string literals — available at source time)
BUF_burx_env="burx.env"

# Multi-fact extension registry (BUK-domain only). Constant value matches
# constant name downcased; the buf_ext_ prefix in the value carries the
# owning module identity so any fact file is recognizable on disk.
BUF_EXT_ALIAS="buf_ext_alias"

# Write a named fact-file to both output and temp directories.
# Dies if either copy already exists (double-write indicates a bug).
# Args: <filename> <value>
buf_write_fact_single() {
  local -r z_filename="$1"
  local -r z_value="$2"
  local -r z_output_path="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${z_filename}"
  local -r z_temp_path="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/${z_filename}"
  test ! -f "${z_output_path}" || { echo "FATAL: buf_write_fact_single: preexists in output dir: ${z_output_path}" >&2; return 1; }
  test ! -f "${z_temp_path}"   || { echo "FATAL: buf_write_fact_single: preexists in temp dir: ${z_temp_path}" >&2; return 1; }
  printf '%s\n' "${z_value}" > "${z_output_path}"
  printf '%s\n' "${z_value}" > "${z_temp_path}"
}

# Write a multi-element fact-file (<file_root>.<ext>) to both output and
# temp directories. Dies if either copy already exists. Extension is an
# opaque pass-through — no validation, no closed-set enforcement.
# Args: <file_root> <ext> <value>
buf_write_fact_multi() {
  local -r z_root="$1"
  local -r z_ext="$2"
  local -r z_value="$3"
  local -r z_filename="${z_root}.${z_ext}"
  local -r z_output_path="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${z_filename}"
  local -r z_temp_path="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/${z_filename}"
  test ! -f "${z_output_path}" || { echo "FATAL: buf_write_fact_multi: preexists in output dir: ${z_output_path}" >&2; return 1; }
  test ! -f "${z_temp_path}"   || { echo "FATAL: buf_write_fact_multi: preexists in temp dir: ${z_temp_path}" >&2; return 1; }
  printf '%s\n' "${z_value}" > "${z_output_path}"
  printf '%s\n' "${z_value}" > "${z_temp_path}"
}

# eof

input.description: Check fact-multi conventions and Rust reader

content.content:
---
# Fact-file extension tinder — multi-fact registry for buf_write_fact_multi.
# Producers emit "<basename>.<extension>" via filesystem-as-data-bus pattern;
# consumers walk fact files in BURD_OUTPUT_DIR / BURD_TEMP_DIR keyed on extension.
# Roster extensions composed from earlier tinder (BCG tinder-on-tinder).
RBCC_fact_ext_depot="depot"
RBCC_fact_ext_depot_project="depot-project"
RBCC_fact_ext_roster_retriever="${RBCC_verb_roster}-${RBCC_role_retriever}"
RBCC_fact_ext_roster_director="${RBCC_verb_roster}-${RBCC_role_director}"

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbcc_*)

zrbcc_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbcc already kindled"
  test -n "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR:-}" || buc_die "BURC_TOOLS_DIR not set - rbcc requires BURC environment"

  # Kindle constants (depend on runtime state)
  readonly RBCC_KIT_DIR="${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}/${RBBC_kit_subdir}"

  # Curl timeout bounds — all actionable curl sites use these
  readonly RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC=10
  readonly RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC=60

  # Bottle test fixtures sleep after rbob_charge to let services (Jupyter, PlantUML)
  # initialize before HTTP assertions. A readiness poll would be precise but adds
  # dynamic state and retry logic that bash handles poorly. Fixed delay is reliable
  # if generous — test suite runtime matters less than test reliability.
  # Increase if services grow heavier.
  readonly RBCC_BOTTLE_TEST_READINESS_DELAY_SEC=30

  readonly ZRBCC_KINDLED=1
--- rbgp_Payor.sh fact-multi pattern ---
# query "displayName:RBGC-DEPOT*" returns ACTIVE and DELETE_REQUESTED
# projects together (no billing-account enumeration required).  Moniker is
# derived by stripping the "${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} " prefix from each
# project's displayName.  Per-moniker fact files named
# "<moniker>.${RBCC_fact_ext_depot}" (state) and
# "<moniker>.${RBCC_fact_ext_depot_project}" (project_id) are written via
# buf_write_fact_multi; consumers walk the emitted files in BURD_OUTPUT_DIR /
# BURD_TEMP_DIR (filesystem is the data bus).
#
# No RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID required for enumeration; no Mason SA IAM scan.
# The displayName anchor is the sole discriminator.  Projects whose
# displayName does not parse to a valid RBRR-canonical moniker are skipped.
#
# Paginated via nextPageToken; per-page infixes keep response files distinct.
#
# Single-shot per dispatch — buf_write_fact_multi dies on preexist.
#
# Args: <oauth_access_token>
zrbgp_depot_state_emit() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_token="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_token}" || buc_die "zrbgp_depot_state_emit: token required"

  local -r z_search_query="displayName:${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX}*"
  local -r z_search_url_base="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects:search"

  # Anchor prefix string used inside loop for displayName stripping.
  local -r z_display_prefix="${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} "

  # Per-iteration synthesized locals (BCG exception 2 — declare outside, assign inside)
  local z_search_page=1
  local z_search_page_token=""
  local z_search_url=""
  local z_query_enc=""
  local z_tok_enc=""

content.text:
You're right — and there's a clean precedent. Stopping the parser fix.

**The right architecture:**

1. **Producer**: `rbfl_audit_hallmarks` emits per-hallmark fact files via `buf_write_fact_multi <hallmark> audit-hallmark <hallmark>` — one file per discovered hallmark. Same shape as `rbgp_depot_state_emit` (line 145-180 in rbgp_Payor.sh) which uses fact-multi for depot enumeration.
2. **Constant**: Add `RBCC_fact_ext_audit_hallmark="audit-hallmark"` to `rbcc_Constants.sh` (alongside `RBCC_fact_ext_depot`, `RBCC_fact_ext_roster_*`).
3. **Consumer (Rust)**: Add `rbtdri_enumerate_burv_facts(result, extension) -> Vec<String>` to `rbtdri_invocation.rs` — reads `BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`, lists files matching `*.<extension>`, returns sorted basenames.
4. **Test code**: Replace `zrbtdrc_audit_parse(stdout)` with `rbtdri_enumerate_burv_facts(audit_result, RBCC_fact_ext_audit_hallmark)`. Drop `zrbtdrc_is_hallmark_id` and `zrbtdrc_audit_parse` entirely.

**Trade-offs:**
- ✅ Eliminates fragile stdout parsing (the immediate failure)
- ✅ Aligns with BBAAO fact-multi foundation already laid for SA roster + depot enumeration
- ✅ Audit becomes machine-consumable for any future tool, not just this test
- ⚠️ Touches production foundry code (`rbfl_audit_hallmarks`) — fact emission addition
- ⚠️ Adds Rust reader infrastructure (`rbtdri_enumerate_burv_facts`) — small but new
- ⚠️ Mild scope expansion beyond BBAA7's stated docket (which spoke to airgap-preflight tightening but not audit-fact emission)

**Question**: Is audit-fact-emission in BBAA7's scope, or do you want to slate a separate pace for it (and revert my parser changes for now)?

My read: it's load-bearing for BBAA7's hallmark-lifecycle case correctness — without it, the case can only be brittle. Justified inside this pace, similar to how the busybox vessel-config flip became in-scope when the airgap-preflight surfaced it.

local z_count=0
  local z_prev=""
  local z_line=""
  local z_h=""
  while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
    test -n "${z_line}" || continue
    z_h="${z_line%% *}"
    test -n "${z_h}" || continue
    if test "${z_h}" != "${z_prev}"; then
      printf "  %s\n" "${z_h}"
      z_count=$(( z_count + 1 ))
      z_prev="${z_h}"
    fi
  done < "${ZRBFC_PACKAGE_LIST_FILE}"

  echo ""
  buc_info "Total hallmarks: ${z_count}"
  buc_success "Audit complete"
}

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Common Constants - File paths and naming conventions

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBCC_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbcc multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBCC_SOURCED=1

# Source RBBC bootstrap constants (source-time, before kindle)
test -n "${BURD_CONFIG_DIR:-}" || buc_die "BURD_CONFIG_DIR not set - rbcc requires launcher environment"
source "${BURD_CONFIG_DIR}/rbbc_constants.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source rbbc_constants.sh"

# Literal constants (pure string literals, no variable expansion — available at source time)
RBCC_rbrs_file="../station-files/rbrs.env"
RBCC_rbrn_file="rbrn.env"
RBCC_rbra_file="rbra.env"
RBCC_rbro_file="rbro.env"
RBCC_role_governor="governor"
RBCC_role_retriever="retriever"
RBCC_role_director="director"
RBCC_role_payor="payor"
RBCC_role_assay="assay"
RBCC_role_mason="mason"
RBCC_onboarding_nameplate="tadmor"

# Cult-verb tinder — used by SA-management surface (invest/divest/roster) and
# composed into fact-extension constants below.
RBCC_verb_invest="invest"
RBCC_verb_divest="divest"
RBCC_verb_roster="roster"

# Fact-file extension tinder — multi-fact registry for buf_write_fact_multi.
# Producers emit "<basename>.<extension>" via filesystem-as-data-bus pattern;
# consumers walk fact files in BURD_OUTPUT_DIR / BURD_TEMP_DIR keyed on extension.
# Roster extensions composed from earlier tinder (BCG tinder-on-tinder).
RBCC_fact_ext_depot="depot"
RBCC_fact_ext_depot_project="depot-project"
RBCC_fact_ext_roster_retriever="${RBCC_verb_roster}-${RBCC_role_retriever}"
RBCC_fact_ext_roster_director="${RBCC_verb_roster}-${RBCC_role_director}"

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbcc_*)

zrbcc_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbcc already kindled"
  test -n "${BURC_TOOLS_DIR:-}" || buc_die "BURC_TOOLS_DIR not set - rbcc requires BURC environment"

  # Kindle constants (depend on runtime state)
  readonly RBCC_KIT_DIR="${BURC_TOOLS_DIR}/${RBBC_kit_subdir}"

  # Curl timeout bounds — all actionable curl sites use these
  readonly RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC=10
  readonly RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC=60

  # Bottle test fixtures sleep after rbob_charge to let services (Jupyter, PlantUML)
  # initialize before HTTP assertions. A readiness poll would be precise but adds
  # dynamic state and retry logic that bash handles poorly. Fixed delay is reliable
  # if generous — test suite runtime matters less than test reliability.
  # Increase if services grow heavier.
  readonly RBCC_BOTTLE_TEST_READINESS_DELAY_SEC=30

  readonly ZRBCC_KINDLED=1
}

zrbcc_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbcc not kindled - call zrbcc_kindle first"
}

# eof

params.intent: Replace stdout-parsing in hallmark-lifecycle with the fact-multi machine-contract path. The first live run of the fixture surfaced that rbw-iah's stdout includes BUW pre-banner, ANSI-coded buc_step logging, and table chrome alongside the actual hallmark rows; parsing that display stream is fragile and a code smell against the established BBAAO fact-multi foundation already used by SA roster (rbgg_Governor.sh) and depot enumeration (rbgp_Payor.sh). Producer side: rbfl_audit_hallmarks now calls buf_write_fact_multi inside its dedup-check block, emitting one <hallmark>.audit-hallmark file per discovered hallmark to BURD_OUTPUT_DIR / BURD_TEMP_DIR (filesystem-as-data-bus). The display printf stays unchanged — fact emission is purely additive. RBCC_fact_ext_audit_hallmark='audit-hallmark' added to rbcc_Constants.sh's fact-extension tinder registry alongside RBCC_fact_ext_depot, RBCC_fact_ext_roster_*, etc. Consumer side: rbtdri_invocation.rs gains rbtdri_read_burv_facts_multi(result, extension) -> Result<Vec<String>, String> — enumerates burv_output/current/ for files matching *.<extension>, returns sorted basename-without-extension list. rbtdrc_hallmark_lifecycle's three audit calls (baseline, post-ordain, final) now read structured facts via this enumerator instead of parsing display stdout. zrbtdrc_audit_parse and zrbtdrc_is_hallmark_id helpers deleted (no longer needed). Constant RBTDRC_FACT_EXT_AUDIT_HALLMARK mirrors the bash side. Architectural alignment: audit-hallmarks is now machine-consumable for any future tool, not just this test — same shape SA roster already publishes per-identity. tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh clean. shellcheck clean on rbcc_Constants.sh and rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh.

content.text:
**Surprise debugged**: `rbfl_rekon_hallmark` line 616 calls `buc_die` with `Hallmark not found: <hallmark>` when the package doesn't exist in the registry. So rekon's contract for absent hallmarks is "die with error" — not "succeed with all-no". My step 6 expected the wrong shape.

Let me check the exact message and make the step 6 assertion match the actual contract.

local z_h=""
  local z_b=""

    test -n "${z_line}" || continue

params.intent: Fix step 6 of hallmark-lifecycle to match the actual rekon contract for abjured hallmarks. Initial assertion expected rekon to succeed (exit 0) and report all five ark basenames as not-yes. Live run surfaced the real contract: rbfl_rekon_hallmark calls buc_die with 'Hallmark not found: <hallmark>' (rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh:616) when the hallmark's package subtree is absent from GAR, exiting non-zero. This is the cleaner contract — abjure either removes the entire registry entry or it didn't run; there's no 'partial state' for rekon to enumerate. Step 6 now positively asserts that exit-non-zero shape: invoke rekon, expect exit_code != 0 AND stderr contains 'Hallmark not found: <hallmark>'. Three failure paths surfaced explicitly (succeeded when should have died, died with wrong message, invocation failure). Combines with step 7's audit-list-restored assertion as defense-in-depth: rekon checks the package-level lookup, audit checks the registry-level enumeration, both must confirm absence. Producer string is hardcoded with a comment naming rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh as the source of truth; future drift breaks this test, which is intentional coupling.

content.text:
Found it — `BURX_EXIT_STATUS=1` confirms rekon died correctly. But the buc_die message went to BUW's transcript file, not to the actual stderr stream (`result.stderr` is empty). The Rust caller sees only what BUW wrote to its own fd 1/2.

Two ways to fix:
- **(A) Drop the message-content check, rely on exit_code != 0** — exit-non-zero IS the meaningful contract; rekon's only way to die on a known-good path is "hallmark not found"
- **(B) Check stdout+stderr combined for the marker** — adds noise tolerance but couples to BUW output routing

Going with (A) — cleaner. The exit code is the load-bearing signal; checking message content was over-coupling to a launcher implementation detail.

1	<!--

3	SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
4	See LICENSE for terms.
5	-->
6	
7	# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
12	
13	The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.
14	
15	Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.
16	
17	Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
18	
19	The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
20	
21	### Load-Bearing Complexity
22	
23	Every element in a system — every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction — must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.
24	
25	This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"
26	
27	Concrete examples from this codebase:
28	
29	- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing — extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
30	- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation — same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
31	- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** — the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.
32	
33	Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.
34	
35	## Module Architecture
36	
37	Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
38	
39	```bash
40	«prefix»_«name».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
41	«prefix»_cli.sh (entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
42	```
43	
44	### CLI as Module Gateway
45	
46	**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
47	
48	**Pattern**:
49	```
50	External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
51	```
52	
53	**Why this matters**:
54	- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
55	- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
56	- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
57	- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality
58	
59	**Anti-pattern**:
60	```bash
61	# ❌ External script kindling directly
62	source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
63	zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
64	rbob_charge
65	```
66	
67	**Correct pattern**:
68	```bash
69	# ✅ External script delegates to CLI
70	exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
71	```
72	
73	**What counts as "external code"**:
74	- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
75	- TabTargets and launchers
76	- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
77	- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules
78	
79	**What can call kindle**:
80	- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
81	- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
82	- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior
83	
84	**Filesystem marker**: The `_cli.sh` suffix and the executable bit must agree. Within a BCG module directory:
85	
86	- Every `*_cli.sh` MUST be executable
87	- Every other `*.sh` MUST NOT be executable
88	
89	This makes the gateway role visible to `ls -l`: a workbench, testbench, or implementation module carrying `+x` is a bug — direct execution bypasses the CLI's `furnish()` and the kindle graph it owns. Tabtargets in `tt/` and shipped tool binaries are not BCG modules and stand outside this rule.
90	
91	### Module-CLI Prefix Unity
92	
93	**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
94	
95	When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
96	only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
97	function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
98	dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
99	
100	### Regime Module Archetype
101	
102	A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields — the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
103	
104	| Component | Location | Purpose |
105	|-----------|----------|---------|
106	| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
107	| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
108	| `z«prefix»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
109	| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
110	| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` — explained per-field display |
111	| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` — diagnostic dump |
112	
113	Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
114	
115	**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
116	
117	Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
118	
119	---
120	
121	## Function Patterns
122	
123	### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)
124	
125	| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
126	|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
127	| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
128	| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
129	| `z«prefix»_enforce`  | Implementation | `z«prefix»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
130	| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
131	| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
132	| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |
133	
134	**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
135	
136	**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
137	
138	**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
139	
140	**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
141	
142	### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)
143	
144	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
145	
146	| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
147	|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
148	| Public          | `«prefix»_«command»` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
149	| Internal helper | `z«prefix»_«name»`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |
150	
151	---
152	
153	## File Templates
154	
155	### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright
156	
157	```bash
158	#!/bin/bash
159	#
160	# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
161	#
162	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
163	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
164	# You may obtain a copy of the License at
165	#
166	#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
167	#
168	# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
169	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
170	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
171	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
172	# limitations under the License.
173	#
174	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
175	#
176	```
177	
178	### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables
179	
180	Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():
181	
182	| Variable | Source | Value |
183	|----------|--------|-------|
184	| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
185	| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
186	| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |
187	
188	These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.
189	
190	### Template 2: CLI Entry Point
191	
192	BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars — furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.
193	
194	```bash
195	# «shebang»
196	# «copyright»
197	
198	set -euo pipefail
199	
200	source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
201	
202	z«prefix»_furnish() {
203	
204	  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
205	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
206	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
207	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
208	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
209	
210	  # Source all dependencies
211	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
212	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"
213	
214	  # Source regime files, if any
215	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
216	
217	  z«prefix»_kindle
218	  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
219	}
220	
221	buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"
222	
223	# eof
224	```
225	
226	### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
227	
228	The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
229	
230	**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
231	
232	```bash
233	z«prefix»_furnish() {
234	  local z_command="${1:-}"
235	
236	  # Document environment variables
237	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
238	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
239	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
240	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
241	
242	  # Always source light dependencies
243	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
244	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"
245	
246	  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
247	  case "${z_command}" in
248	    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
249	      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
250	      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
251	      zheavy_kindle
252	      ;;
253	    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
254	      # These commands work with config only
255	      ;;
256	  esac
257	
258	  z«prefix»_kindle
259	}
260	```
261	
262	**Key points:**
263	- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
264	- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
265	- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
266	- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
267	- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
268	
269	**Benefits:**
270	- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
271	- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
272	- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
273	- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure
274	
275	### Template 3: Implementation Module
276	
277	```bash
278	# «shebang»
279	# «copyright»
280	
281	set -euo pipefail
282	
283	# Multiple inclusion detection
284	test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
285	Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1
286	
287	# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
288	# «PREFIX»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
289	# «PREFIX»_some_prefix="prefix_"
290	
291	######################################################################
292	# Internal Functions (z«prefix»_*)
293	
294	z«prefix»_kindle() {
295	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
296	
297	  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
298	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
299	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
300	  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps
301	
302	  # Validate module specific environment
303	  buv_file_exists "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present
304	
305	  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
306	  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"
307	
308	  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
309	  «PREFIX»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"
310	
311	  # Kindle constants (internal)
312	  Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
313	  Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_result.json"
314	  Z«PREFIX»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
315	  Z«PREFIX»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use
316	
317	  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
318	  Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"
319	
320	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
321	}
322	
323	z«prefix»_sentinel() {
324	  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
325	}
326	
327	# Helper functions follow
328	
329	######################################################################
330	# External Functions («prefix»_*)
331	
332	«prefix»_«command»() {
333	  z«prefix»_sentinel
334	
335	  local z_param1="${1:-}"
336	  local z_param2="${2:-}"
337	
338	  # Documentation block
339	  buc_doc_brief "«one_line_description»"
340	  buc_doc_param "«required_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
341	  buc_doc_oparm "«optional_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
342	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
343	
344	  # Implementation
345	  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"
346	
347	  buc_step "«user_visible_milestone»"
348	  buc_log_args "«detailed_progress_information»"
349	  [command] || buc_die "«failure_explanation»"
350	
351	  buc_success "«success_message»"
352	}
353	```
354	
355	**Kindle constant — anti-pattern:**
356	
357	```bash
358	# ❌ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
359	z«prefix»_setup() {
360	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
361	}
362	
363	# ✅ Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
364	z«prefix»_kindle() {
365	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
366	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
367	}
368	```
369	
370	**Tinder vs kindle constant — choosing correctly:**
371	
372	```bash
373	# ✅ Tinder constant: pure string, available at source time
374	«PREFIX»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name — no kindle needed
375	
376	# ✅ Tinder-on-tinder composition: expansion of earlier tinder constant
377	«PREFIX»_role_director="director"
378	«PREFIX»_fact_ext_director="ext-${«PREFIX»_role_director}"  # resolves at source time
379	
380	# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
381	«PREFIX»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name — kindle required
382	
383	# ❌ Wrong: runtime variable expansion in tinder position
384	«PREFIX»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: BURD_TEMP_DIR is runtime — must be kindle
385	
386	# ❌ Wrong: computation in tinder position
387	«PREFIX»_color_red=$(z«prefix»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() — must be in kindle
388	
389	# ❌ Wrong: expansion of kindle constant in tinder position
390	«PREFIX»_derived="${«PREFIX»_KINDLED_VAR}/sub"  # VIOLATION: KINDLED_VAR available only after kindle
391	```
392	
393	## Variable Handling (General Rules)
394	
395	### Expansion Requirements
396	
397	**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
398	```bash
399	"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
400	"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle

# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference

## Core Philosophy

This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.

The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.

Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.

Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.

The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.

### Load-Bearing Complexity

Every element in a system — every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction — must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.

This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"

Concrete examples from this codebase:

- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing — extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation — same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** — the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.

Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.

## Module Architecture

Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.

```bash
«prefix»_«name».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
«prefix»_cli.sh (entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
```

### CLI as Module Gateway

**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.

**Pattern**:
```
External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
```

**Why this matters**:
- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality

**Anti-pattern**:
```bash
# ❌ External script kindling directly
source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
rbob_charge
```

**Correct pattern**:
```bash
# ✅ External script delegates to CLI
exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
```

**What counts as "external code"**:
- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
- TabTargets and launchers
- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules

**What can call kindle**:
- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior

**Filesystem marker**: The `_cli.sh` suffix and the executable bit must agree. Within a BCG module directory:

- Every `*_cli.sh` MUST be executable
- Every other `*.sh` MUST NOT be executable

This makes the gateway role visible to `ls -l`: a workbench, testbench, or implementation module carrying `+x` is a bug — direct execution bypasses the CLI's `furnish()` and the kindle graph it owns. Tabtargets in `tt/` and shipped tool binaries are not BCG modules and stand outside this rule.

### Module-CLI Prefix Unity

**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).

When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
dependencies based on which command is being invoked.

### Regime Module Archetype

A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields — the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:

| Component | Location | Purpose |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
| `z«prefix»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` — explained per-field display |
| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` — diagnostic dump |

Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.

**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.

Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.

---

## Function Patterns

### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)

| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
| `z«prefix»_enforce`  | Implementation | `z«prefix»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |

**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.

**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.

**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.

**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.

### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)

This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.

| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
| Public          | `«prefix»_«command»` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
| Internal helper | `z«prefix»_«name»`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |

---

## File Templates

### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
```

### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables

Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():

| Variable | Source | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |

These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.

### Template 2: CLI Entry Point

BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars — furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.

```bash
# «shebang»
# «copyright»

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"

z«prefix»_furnish() {

  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  # Source all dependencies
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"

  # Source regime files, if any
  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"

  z«prefix»_kindle
  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
}

buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"

# eof
```

### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)

The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.

**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.

```bash
z«prefix»_furnish() {
  local z_command="${1:-}"

  # Document environment variables
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  # Always source light dependencies
  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"

  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
  case "${z_command}" in
    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
      zheavy_kindle
      ;;
    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
      # These commands work with config only
      ;;
  esac

  z«prefix»_kindle
}
```

**Key points:**
- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies

**Benefits:**
- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure

### Template 3: Implementation Module

```bash
# «shebang»
# «copyright»

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1

# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
# «PREFIX»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
# «PREFIX»_some_prefix="prefix_"

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (z«prefix»_*)

z«prefix»_kindle() {
  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"

  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps

  # Validate module specific environment
  buv_file_exists "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present

  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"

  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
  «PREFIX»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"

  # Kindle constants (internal)
  Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
  Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_result.json"
  Z«PREFIX»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
  Z«PREFIX»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use

  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
  Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"

  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}

z«prefix»_sentinel() {
  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
}

# Helper functions follow

######################################################################
# External Functions («prefix»_*)

«prefix»_«command»() {
  z«prefix»_sentinel

  local z_param1="${1:-}"
  local z_param2="${2:-}"

  # Documentation block
  buc_doc_brief "«one_line_description»"
  buc_doc_param "«required_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
  buc_doc_oparm "«optional_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  # Implementation
  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"

  buc_step "«user_visible_milestone»"
  buc_log_args "«detailed_progress_information»"
  [command] || buc_die "«failure_explanation»"

  buc_success "«success_message»"
}
```

**Kindle constant — anti-pattern:**

```bash
# ❌ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
z«prefix»_setup() {
  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
}

# ✅ Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
z«prefix»_kindle() {
  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
}
```

**Tinder vs kindle constant — choosing correctly:**

```bash
# ✅ Tinder constant: pure string, available at source time
«PREFIX»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name — no kindle needed

# ✅ Tinder-on-tinder composition: expansion of earlier tinder constant
«PREFIX»_role_director="director"
«PREFIX»_fact_ext_director="ext-${«PREFIX»_role_director}"  # resolves at source time

# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
«PREFIX»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name — kindle required

# ❌ Wrong: runtime variable expansion in tinder position
«PREFIX»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: BURD_TEMP_DIR is runtime — must be kindle

# ❌ Wrong: computation in tinder position
«PREFIX»_color_red=$(z«prefix»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() — must be in kindle

# ❌ Wrong: expansion of kindle constant in tinder position
«PREFIX»_derived="${«PREFIX»_KINDLED_VAR}/sub"  # VIOLATION: KINDLED_VAR available only after kindle
```

## Variable Handling (General Rules)

### Expansion Requirements

**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
```bash
"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle

file.content:
# Multi-line print function with verbosity control
# Sends output to stderr to avoid interfering with stdout returns
zbuc_print() {
  local min_verbosity="$1"
  shift

  # Always print if min_verbosity is -1, otherwise check BURE_VERBOSE
  if test "${min_verbosity}" -eq -1 || test "${BURE_VERBOSE:-0}" -ge "${min_verbosity}"; then
    while test $# -gt 0; do
      if test -n "${ZBUC_CONTEXT}"; then
        printf '%b%s%b %s\n' "${BUC_gray}" "${ZBUC_CONTEXT}" "${BUC_reset}" "$1" >&2
      else
        echo "$1" >&2
      fi
      shift
    done
  fi
}

# Core logging implementation - always reads from stdin
zbuc_log() {
  test -n "${BURD_TRANSCRIPT:-}" || return 0

  local z_prefix="$1"
  local z_rest_prefix="$2"
  local z_outfile="${BURD_TRANSCRIPT}"

  while IFS= read -r z_line; do
    printf '%s%s\n' "${z_prefix}" "${z_line}" >> "${z_outfile}"
    z_prefix="${z_rest_prefix}"
  done
}
